[JS Weird World] No.42 call(), apply() and bind() — Controlling this Yourself
This time we're looking at three JavaScript methods —
. And they all connect back to stuff we've already covered.
Before we get into them though, we need to remember what a first-class function is. Way back at the start we saw that a function is a special kind of thing:
It can have a name, or no name at all (an anonymous function)
It has a code block where you write code, and that code can be invoked
an object, so it can have its own properties and methods
and the arguments you pass in.

Remember, bind only copies — it doesn't run. But the arguments you hand it become permanent argument values on the copied function. So putting 2 as the first argument means a is always 2.

Time to dig into three methods every JavaScript function carries around: call(), apply() and bind(). They all tie back to this and the arguments you pass in. We'll see how bind copies a function and locks this, how call runs it on the spot, how apply does the same but takes an array, and finish with real use cases like function borrowing and function currying.
